title: Bash is not reading ~/.bashrc when run as a non-login shell
body:
/kind bug

**Description**

Due to https://github.com/containers/toolbox/issues/1076, I am trying to figure out how `PATH` gets set for processes invoked inside a Podman container.  I cannot understand why invoking `bash` (as a non-login shell) doesn't read the start-up files in `$HOME`, but invoking `bash --login` does.

While it's true that Bash is expected to read a different set of files depending on whether it's a login shell or not, on Fedora, the start-up scripts are set up in a way that `~/.bashrc` and `/etc/bashrc` are always read.

So far, I have seen `DefaultEnvVariables()` in `pkg/env/env.go`, but further investigation is getting hindered by things like `pstree(1)` not being very helpful.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

Notice how `bash` doesn't pick up anything from `$HOME`, and has only the defaults from `DefaultEnvVariables()`:
```
$ podman run --interactive --security-opt label=disable --tty --userns=keep-id --volume $HOME:$HOME:rslave fedora:36 bash -c 'echo $PATH'
/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin
```

On a recent Fedora, one would expect to see `~/.local/bin` and `~/bin` in `PATH`, because `~/.bashrc` adds them.

However, `bash --login` does pick them up from `$HOME`:
```
$ podman run --interactive --security-opt label=disable --tty --userns=keep-id --volume $HOME:$HOME:rslave fedora:36 bash -l -c 'echo $PATH'
dircolors: no SHELL environment variable, and no shell type option given
/home/rishi/.local/bin:/home/rishi/bin:/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin
```

I am puzzled because, on Fedora, the start-up scripts are set up in a way that `~/.bashrc` and `/etc/bashrc` are always read, and I am wondering if that message about a missing `SHELL` is related to this.

**Describe the results you expected:**

I expected `bash` (as a non-login shell) to read `~/.bashrc` and pick up `~/.local/bin` and `~/bin` in `PATH`.

**Output of `podman version`:**

```

Version:      4.2.1

Go Version:   go1.18.5
Built:        Wed Sep  7 21:58:19 2022

```

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

I am using Fedora 36 Workstation.

comments.body:
I don't see how this a podman issue?
Podman starts the process with the configured env. If bash decides to read bashrc or not is up to that process not podman.
I suspect that the problem is that you use `-c`. If I just run `bash`, then execute `echo $PATH` it shows the expected path.

From the bash man page:
>  When an interactive shell that is not a login shell is started, bash reads and executes commands from ~/.bashrc, if that file exists.  This may be inhibited by using the --norc option.  The --rcfile file option will force bash to read and execute  commands  from  file  instead  of
       ~/.bashrc.

Using `-c` means it is not interactive and thus does not read ~/.bashrc. At least this is how I interpret it.

Yes, you are right, I overlooked this part in the Bash manual:
```
When  bash is started non-interactively, to run a shell script, for
example, it looks for the variable BASH_ENV in the environment,
expands its value if it appears there, and uses the expanded value
as the name of a file to read  and  execute.
```

> I don't see how this a podman issue?

My apologies, I should have been clearer.  I didn't mean to say that it was definitely a Podman bug.

I was just trying to understand the exact sequence of steps on the way to a process getting invoked inside a container.  From Bash's built-in defaults, to systemd, to GDM, to Podman's built-in defaults and the OCI image's built-in defaults, there seems to be an impressive number of places where `PATH` can get set on a stock graphical Fedora Workstation session.  :)

> Podman starts the process with
> the configured env. If bash decides to read bashrc or not is up to that
> process not podman. I suspect that the problem is that you use `-c`.
> If I just run `bash`, then execute `echo $PATH` it shows the expected path.

Yes, you are right.  It's the `-c` that's the problem.  Toolbx has another layer because it goes through `capsh ... -- -c ...`, and in trying to cut through that I forgot about the `-c`.  Damn, I feel stupid.

Anyway, thanks for clearing it up!
